The Story

About eightysix

Eightysix started because I got bored of menus that never change. In kitchen slang, "eightysix" means "sold out," which is what happens when the same six items sit on a menu for a year. I wanted a dessert that behaved like the foods we grew up with, Twisties, ice bean, kaya toast, except now I have the equipment and the stubbornness to take it apart and rebuild it properly. No shortcuts, even when the troublesome route adds three extra days.

To me, eightysix is a dessert we never imagined could be a dessert. It's a non-binary kind of dessert. You could call it fusion, a mix of this and a little bit of that, but for me it represents what could be or what might have been. Even though the English isn't perfect, why not try to make it work? Science says so. It pairs well with A and B because their flavor profiles match scientifically.

I don't create things just because they look good, are presentable, or taste good. I follow the science and what works. That's why I call it non-binary. When things just seamlessly work well together, creation doesn't have to stop there. eightysix is where things end. Things are finished, things are done or sold out. We would never run out of creativity if we base our perspective on a one-dimensional look. We will blind ourselves to the possibility of a new reality.

About the baker

In 2018, I started writing a book. Types Of Bakers. Got four chapters in. Then I stopped writing and started baking wedding cakes instead. Made sense at the time — you can't eat a manuscript, but you can get paid for a three-tier vanilla sponge with sugar flowers. So that's what I did. Client calls, mood boards, "can you make it more like the Pinterest photo," the same six flavors on repeat because that's what sells at a wedding. It paid. It also had nothing to do with why I wanted to write a book about bakers in the first place.

Somewhere in there, the pandemic hit. Everything stopped. And underneath the shutdown, something else stopped too — my own discipline. I wasn't executing. I wasn't building anything. I had the idea buried in me, but not the structure to carry it.

So I lost time. I lost momentum. I lost the version of myself that thought four chapters and a decent instinct for cake would be enough.

What I gained, I didn't see until later: actual industry reps. Technique that only comes from doing the repetitive, unglamorous version of the job first. And discipline I didn't have in 2018 — the kind you only get from being forced to sit still and actually look at what you're doing, instead of just doing it.

That's when the shift happened. Not back to the book. Forward, past it. High-end pastry technique doesn't have to live behind a $400 tasting menu and a dress code. It can live in a home kitchen, four guests at a time, dessert as the whole point instead of the thing that comes after the "real" meal.

That's eightysix. Not a pivot. The thing the book was actually about, built with hands that finally knew what they were doing.

I'm one guy, one kitchen, capped at four seats — and I'm building the only dessert-focused, home-based private dining concept in Singapore. If you want to see what four chapters and a five-year detour turn into, I'll show you.

#story

The Neighbourhood Session

  • Show up, sit down, see who's there.The Social SessionCommunal 7-course dessert omakase, shared table, 3 mocktails included. SGD 148 / pax↗
  • The whole crew. No strangers, no rushing out the door.The Kampung SessionPrivate full table for 4, 7-course omakase — birthdays, celebrations, gatherings. SGD 560 / table↗
  • My call on the night — you just pick sweet or savory.The Suka-Suka Session"As one pleases" — 5 courses, chef's selection, your call on savory/sweet. SGD 118 / pax↗
  • Zero alcohol. Not zero effort.The Kosong SessionAdd-on to any session — 3 non-alcoholic pairing mocktails. BYOB welcome. SGD 38 add-on↗

Social

When we were kids, the whole day revolved around one question: will the same group be at the playground tomorrow? Catching, block-catching, whatever the game was — the game was almost beside the point. It was the excuse. The real thing was showing up and finding your people there again.

Somewhere between then and now, we stopped needing an excuse to sit near strangers. Old folks still do it — the senior hub under the void deck runs on the same logic, same faces, same benches, no agenda. We do it too, at a bar, three drinks in, suddenly best friends with the next table and the bartender and whoever's smoking outside. Give people close proximity and a bit of time, and they'll talk.

So eightysix runs a communal table. Seven courses. You don't pick who you're seated next to — you find out when you sit down. No pressure to book out the whole room, no obligation to perform small talk with people you brought. Just the playground rule again: show up, sit down, see who's there.

Kampung

There's a specific kind of gathering that doesn't need strangers. You already have the group — the same six or eight or twelve people who show up every time, who you've known long enough that you don't perform for each other anymore. The question isn't who's going to be there. It's where you take them.

That's what this table is for. Not the communal seating, not the surprise of who you're sitting next to — the opposite. You book the whole table. No strangers folded in, no fifteen-minute turnover clock ticking because another party's waiting outside. Just your crew, for as long as the seven courses take to get through.

Kampung doesn't mean a place. It means everyone shows up — the whole extended crew, kids included, aunties included, the friend who's basically family included — and nobody's rushing anyone out the door. That used to happen at the void deck, at someone's flat, at whoever's kitchen had enough chairs. I'm just moving it to a dessert table.

If you're already planning to bring the same people together — a birthday, a reunion, a small kids' gathering, whatever the occasion is — bring them here instead of somewhere generic. You get the room to yourselves. You get me at the table with you, walking through each course, not vanishing into a kitchen. And you get it documented properly, if that's something you want for the day.

This one's built for birthdays and small private gatherings specifically:

  • The whole table is yours — no strangers, no rushing to flip the seating for the next booking.
  • Kampung means the whole crew, not just the guest list — kids, aunties, the friend who's basically family.
  • I'm at the table with you through the seven courses, not disappearing behind a kitchen wall.

Suka-Suka

Suka-suka. As one pleases.

Most tastings ask you to make decisions before you've eaten anything — pick this menu, pick that add-on, commit to a direction before you know what you're committing to. Suka-suka skips that. Five courses, my call on the night. You tell me if you're leaning sweet or leaning savory, and that's the only steering wheel you get. Everything else is mine to build.

This is the one with the least structure and the most room for me to move. If something's good this week — an ingredient that just came in right, a technique I've been circling, an idea that hasn't made it onto a "real" menu yet — this is where it shows up. Social has its shape. Kampung has its shape. Suka-suka doesn't, on purpose. It's the closest thing to being handed whatever I actually feel like making, the night you show up.

If Social is about the table full of strangers, and Kampung is about the table full of your people, suka-suka is about trusting the one person who isn't sitting at the table with you deciding for you — the one in the kitchen.

Kosong

Kosong means empty. Plain. Nothing added unless you ask.

By default, that's the table — dessert, courses, no drink program attached, no upsell waiting at the end of the menu. If you want it to stay that way, it stays that way.

But if you want more than water and coffee, kosong is also the name of the opt-in: three non-alcoholic pairings, built to run alongside whichever session you've booked — Social, Kampung, or Suka-Suka. Not a separate experience, just an addition to the one you already picked.

And if what you actually want is your own bottle on the table, bring it. BYOB is fine. I'm not running a program where I need to control what's in your glass — bring what you like, drink what you like, I'll just be cooking.

#neighbourhood-session

The Local Menu

Snacks that used to cost two dollars at a stall, taken apart at the component level and put back together as a plated course. I'm not dressing them up to be fancier than they are — I'm just finally giving them the technique they never got. Every course below gets its own scroll: the photo, the story behind it, and the actual food science behind why it works.

Course — Small Bites, 01/09

Small Bites

The opener: sweet-potato prawn cracker and a ginger-citron doughnut, both built to snap you awake before the real courses start.

Prawn CrackerSweet red sweet potato · Jumbo blue prawn · Yuzu pulp
DoughnutGlutinous rice dough · Ginger citron glaze
Book a session →
[Story]

Prawn crackers came from a red-and-white plastic packet, always slightly stale by the time anyone opened it, and that was fine — that was the point. The doughnut is the roadside kind, sold hot out of a cart, gone in four bites. Small Bites isn't an appetizer course in the fancy-restaurant sense. It's just what you'd be handed first if I fed you like family instead of a guest.

[Why It Works]

Prawn Cracker: Prawn brings two savory "MSG-type" flavor compounds (glutamate + inosinate) at once — together the savory taste gets boosted way more than either alone. Frying the sweet potato browns and crisps its sugar and starch, giving the actual "cracker" crunch. Yuzu is sour citrus, and sour flavors wake your tongue back up before the next bite.

Doughnut: Glutinous rice flour is almost all one kind of starch molecule that's naturally stretchy, which is why the dough chews instead of crumbling. Frying seals a dry crust outside while the inside stays soft. Ginger tricks the same nerve receptors as chili heat, just gently — so it blends with the citrus glaze instead of clashing.

“Not an appetizer — just how I’d feed you first if you were family, not a guest.”
Course — Snack, 02/09

Cup Corn

Charred-sweet corn, silky honeyed cream, and a savory umami caramel underneath — sweetcorn snack logic, plated.

ComponentsTorched corn kernels · Honey diplomat cream · Shiro dashi caramel
Book a session →
[Story]

Cup corn used to mean a plastic cup, a small red plastic spoon, and however much butter the stall auntie felt like giving you that day. Roadside, five minutes, gone before you got back to your bike. I've eaten it standing up more times than sitting down.

[Why It Works]

Torching the corn browns its surface sugars — same chemical reaction as toasting bread or searing a steak — which is why charred corn tastes deeper and roastier than plain boiled corn even though it's barely sweeter on paper. Shiro dashi caramel is basically savory soy-stock flavor melted into a caramel — it's doing that same "two savory flavors stacking" trick as the prawn cracker, just hidden inside something sweet. The cream has gelatin in it, which just means it's thick and clingy enough to coat your tongue slowly — that slow coat is what makes it feel "silky" instead of watery-sweet.

"It was never really about the corn."
Course — Snack, 03/09

Houten Tapioca Chilli

Sweet-heat chili glaze, roasted peanut foam for nutty richness, coconut mousse to cool the whole thing back down.

ComponentsChilli glaze tapioca · Peanut foam · Coconut mousse
Book a session →
[Story]

Chili tapioca pearls were the candy that made you cry a little and go back for more anyway — sweet first, then a slow-building heat that never really peaked until the packet was empty. I kept that exact pacing and gave it an actual off-switch this time: coconut mousse, so you're not just suffering through the last bite for old times' sake.

[Why It Works]

Chili heat (capsaicin) dissolves in fat, not water — that's exactly why coconut mousse, heavy in fat, actually cools your mouth down, while water barely helps. Whipping the peanut mixture into foam packs in more surface area, so you smell and taste the roasted peanut almost instantly, before the chili heat has time to build. Gula melaka tastes deeper and more mineral-y than plain white sugar, which is why the glaze feels richer than a basic sweet-chili sauce.

“This time it has an off-switch.”
Course — Snack, 04/09

Twisties

Sharp cheese cold and melting, curry oil for heat-adjacent aromatics, tomato water for acid and savoriness underneath.

ComponentsCheese ice cream · Curry oil · Tomato water
Book a session →
[Story]

Orange dust on every finger, and you'd wipe it on your jeans because napkins were for people who cared more than you did at age nine. Twisties was never a snack you ate politely. Turning it into an ice cream course meant keeping the mess-logic and losing the dust — same commitment to cheese, none of the stained fingers.

[Why It Works]

Cold temperatures dull how sweet something tastes but barely touch how savory it tastes — so freezing real cheese into ice cream keeps the full cheesy funk without it turning into a sugar bomb. Curry spices' flavor mostly lives in fat, not water, so steeping them in oil pulls out far more real flavor than steeping in water would. Tomato water is strained tomato juice with the pulp removed — all the savory-tangy flavor, none of the chunky weight.

“Same commitment to cheese. None of the stained fingers.”
Course — Breakfast, 05/09

Kaya Toast

Milk-bread-soft and warm, pandan running green and grassy-sweet, kaya butter salty-caramel on the finish.

ComponentsDouble milk shokupan · Pandan syrup · Kaya butter
Book a session →
[Story]

Every kopitoam breakfast in this country runs on the same three things: toast, kaya, and a soft-boiled egg you crack into a saucer with white pepper and soy sauce. I couldn't fit a full egg course into a seven-course dessert menu, so the toast had to carry all of the nostalgia by itself. It does.

[Why It Works]

Milk protein softens the gluten in bread dough while it rises — the literal reason "milk bread" is fluffier than normal bread, not just extra richness. Pandan's smell molecule is the exact same one found naturally in jasmine rice and freshly toasted bread, so it doesn't just smell "green," it smells like bread already. The salt in kaya butter isn't there to taste salty — it blocks your bitter taste buds a bit, making everything around it taste sweeter without more sugar.

“The toast had to carry all of the nostalgia by itself. It does.”
Course — Dessert, 06/09

Hawflake

Tart hawthorn ice against a cold ginger-lemongrass scrape, coconut cream smoothing the landing — sour first, sharp middle, soft finish.

Hawflake SorbetHawthorn flakes · Lemon juice · Sugar
Lemongrass Ginger GranitaFrozen lemongrass · Frozen ginger · Sugar
Coconut Vanilla CreamWhipping cream · Coconut cream · Jasmine flower
Book a session →
[Story]

Hawflake came in a wax-paper roll, pink discs stacked like poker chips, and it was sour enough that eating too many in a row made your jaw ache. I never grew out of liking that specific kind of sour. This is the grown-up version — still hawthorn, still sharp, now sitting next to a granita and a cream that actually know what to do with that sourness instead of just surviving it.

[Why It Works]

Hawthorn (shan zha) is naturally tart on its own, and the lemon juice in the sorbet base doubles down on that same top-note sourness instead of masking it — a small stabilizer keeps the sorbet smooth despite having no dairy fat to slow ice crystals down naturally. The granita gets frozen and scraped differently on purpose, keeping the ice crystals big and rough — that's what gives it a genuinely different, coarser bite right next to the smoother sorbet, the same shaved-ice logic behind Ice Kacang. Coconut cream blended into the whipping cream adds extra fat, and fat is what coats your tongue and buffers a sharp, sour hit — it's the thing standing between "sour dessert" and "dessert that makes you wince." The jasmine is a pinch, not a scoop, because your nose is sensitive enough to register a floral note at a trace amount — any more and it stops tasting like fruit and starts tasting like perfume.

“Now it knows what to do with the sour, instead of just surviving it.”
Course — Dessert, 07/09

Ice Kacang

Shaved-ice cold, deep palm-sugar sweetness, then a salted crunch layer breaking up every spoonful.

ComponentsGula melaka evaporated milk granita · Salted puff rice & feuilletine croquant · Roasted peanuts
Book a session →
[Story]

One bowl, four spoons, and everyone digging toward whatever color they liked best before someone else got there first — that's the actual memory, more than the dessert itself. This version is plated for one, but the instinct to dig in fast before it melts is still exactly the point.

[Why It Works]

Granita is frozen in a way that keeps the ice crystals big and rough on purpose — that's literally what gives it the "shaved ice" scrape instead of turning smooth like sorbet. The crunchy croquant layer is bound with melted chocolate, and chocolate's fat repels water, acting like a raincoat that keeps the granita's meltwater from turning the crunch soggy. Salting the peanuts doesn't make them taste salty on their own — a little salt nearby makes the sweet granita taste sweeter, a taste-bud trick, not more sugar.

“Still exactly the point: dig in before it melts.”
Course — Dessert, 08/09

Rice & Egg

Warm and starchy, black garlic bringing umami-sweet depth, cured spiced yolk adding a savory, almost custardy richness on top.

ComponentsWarm rice pudding · Black garlic chantilly · Spiced egg yolk
Book a session →
[Story]

The last thing before dessert stops pretending to be dinner and just becomes dinner — rice, egg, something savory-dark underneath. It's the dish I'd actually want to eat on a bad day, so it earns its place on a menu built around dishes I'd actually want to eat.

[Why It Works]

Black garlic is regular garlic slow-aged under heat for weeks — that process turns it from sharp and raw into deeply sweet and savory, the same browning reaction that makes toast taste richer than raw bread. Curing the yolk in honey and spices slowly pulls moisture out and firms it up without fully cooking it, so it turns jammy and spreadable instead of hard-boiled. Serving the rice pudding warm keeps its starch soft and creamy; cooled down, that same starch stiffens and turns grainy.

“The dish I’d actually want to eat on a bad day.”
Course — Pairing, 09/09

Mocktails

Four non-alcoholic pairings built to run alongside any session — fermented apple-ginger, pandan-longan-strawberry, a kaya-toast drink, and a hot-honey raspberry orange.

Fermented Apple, Ginger, CinnamonFresh apple juice · Ginger juice · Cinnamon syrup
Pandan, Longan & StrawberryPandan water · Longan-strawberry syrup · Soda
Kaya ToastKaya cream base · Fresh lime juice · Coconut mousse
Sparkling Raspberry OrangeRaspberry iced tea · Hot honey · Burnt cinnamon syrup
Book a session →
[Story]

Kosong means zero — zero alcohol, not zero effort. BYOB is always welcome here, but I wanted a program that didn't treat the non-drinkers at the table as an afterthought handed a glass of water. Each one is built the same way the desserts are: real technique, not just juice and soda.

[Crafting]

Vinegar tastes sour the same way real fermented drinks do, since it's the same acid bacteria naturally produce during fermentation — your tongue reads "this was brewed" in five minutes flat. Pandan's smell is strong but fades fast, which is why it always needs a backup note to hold the glass together. Chili's heat compound dissolves better in honey than in water, so steeping chili in honey actually pulls real heat into the syrup instead of sitting on the surface.

“Zero alcohol. Not zero effort.”
#local-menu

The Cultural Edition

Neighbourhood is where this starts. It's not the whole plan. There's a full culture-season roadmap behind it — Malay, Indian, Chinese, Peranakan, each one its own volume, each one built around the food that actually shows up in a Singapore household rather than what's easy to plate. And eventually, a Rojak edition — the one that doesn't pick a lane, that takes pieces from all of it and puts them on the same table, the way rojak itself does.

But the roadmap isn't fixed in stone from my side alone. If there's a snack from your side — something you grew up on, something that never gets treated as more than what it is — tell me. That's genuinely where the next volume comes from.

How a volume actually gets built

Every culture season ships the same way: Volume 1, then a Volume 2 roughly six months later — not two versions dropped at once, one built on what the first one taught me. The new season format is 10 core courses, five savory then five dessert, bookended by small bites to open and sweet treats to close, outside the count. That's a real structural change from how the current Neighbourhood Menu runs, and it's deliberate — more room to actually build a savory-to-sweet arc instead of stacking desserts back to back.

None of that happens without runway. The promo push for a new season — R&D photos, behind-the-scenes, the actual captions — starts about two months before launch, and that push only has material to work with if reservations are actively happening in the meantime. Which is the honest version of "book a session": it's not just dinner, it's what feeds the next volume's content. Neighbourhood pays for Melayu's promo cycle, Melayu pays for Indian's, and so on down the roadmap.

Melayu

The Melayu Menu — Vol. 1 (~Jun/Jul 2027)

The starting point is "craving food" — the stuff Malay households eat any time of day, that never sits cleanly in savory or sweet: nasi lemak, mee rebus, mee siam. That in-between quality is exactly what makes it interesting to rebuild as dessert. I'm not sweetening a savory dish and calling it done — I'm pulling apart what actually makes mee rebus taste like mee rebus (the thick sweet-potato curry gravy, the yellow egg noodle, the sambal on the side) and rebuilding those parts with pastry technique instead of a wok.

Still genuinely in R&D, not locked: a sambal ice cream paired against a mee rebus reinterpretation is the current direction I'm circling. Even the noodle isn't settled — boil-and-blend gives you that starchy, glossy texture the egg noodle has; frying gives you something snappier and more textured. Both are on the table. I'd rather show you the actual mess of figuring it out than pretend this is finished.

Why start here at all, out of every craving food that exists: because mee rebus and mee siam already do half my job for me. A gravy that's simultaneously starchy, sweet, savory, and sour is basically a dessert's flavor architecture wearing a savory costume — the sambal on the side is doing the same "sharp thing that cuts through richness" job that yuzu or lime does in the desserts already on the current menu. I'm not importing a foreign logic into Malay food, I'm pointing out the logic was already there.

இந்திய

The Indian Menu — Vol. 1 (timeframe TBD)

Same lens as Melayu, pointed at a different pantry: the everyday craving food, not the banquet version. Think roti prata, teh tarik, thosai, the stuff sold off a cart at 11pm — not the stuff plated at a wedding. I haven't started R&D on this one yet, so there's no dish to name here. What I can tell you is the method won't change: take something that's already loved exactly as it is, break it into its actual components, and see what happens when those components get pastry technique instead of a kadai.

What I keep coming back to, even before R&D starts: teh tarik is already a technique built entirely around texture — the "pulling" is there specifically to aerate the tea and build a foam head, which is a dessert move (think of any whipped, frothed, or foamed element on the current menu) dressed up as a drink-making ritual. Prata's whole appeal is a laminated, layered dough — the exact same structural idea behind a doughnut or a croissant, just cooked flat on a griddle instead of fried or baked. The overlap is already there in the technique, before I've touched a single ingredient.

中华

The Chinese Menu — Vol. 1 (timeframe TBD)

Comes after Indian in the roadmap, same reason it comes after and not before — order isn't random, it's just queued. The candidates in my head right now are the hawker-stall staples that Singaporean-Chinese households actually crave on a Tuesday, not banquet dishes — but nothing's been tested yet, so I'm not going to name a dish and lock myself into it before the R&D even starts. When it does, it'll show up here the same way Melayu did: messy, unconfirmed, updated as it moves.

The honest reason I haven't jumped ahead and started testing early: every volume so far has taught me something about the last one, and Chinese craving food covers such a wide range — teochew porridge sides, bak kwa, the whole kopitiam breakfast set — that I'd rather walk in with the Melayu and Indian lessons already banked than guess now and redo the work later. Slower start, fewer wasted ingredients.

峇峇娘惹

The Peranakan Menu — Vol. 1 (timeframe TBD)

The newest addition to the roadmap, and arguably the closest one to what eightysix already is — Peranakan food is already a mashup, already the product of two cultures sitting at the same table and refusing to pick one. That's basically the whole eightysix premise applied to a cuisine that got there first. No R&D has started, no dish is picked, but this is the volume I'm personally most impatient to get to.

Part of why it's last and not first, even with how excited I am for it: I want three other volumes' worth of technique behind me before I touch a cuisine that's already this layered. Kueh alone is a masterclass in texture — gelatinous, starchy, coconut-forward, often built in visible strata — and I'd rather bring a fuller toolkit to it than rush in early and undersell it.

#cultural-edition

Pick a session.
Let me handle dessert.

Pick a session. That's the only real decision. Everything after that is mine to handle. I'm not staffing a floor — there's me, in a home kitchen, cooking for whoever's booked that slot. Four seats, one cook, one session at a time. Get the format right and the rest takes care of itself.

Book now ↓

Terms & Conditions

Before the deposit goes in, here's what you're actually agreeing to — the deposit terms, the reschedule/cancellation window, and everything else that matters before you book.

Payment & Deposits

  • Full payment is encouraged.
  • A 50% deposit at checkout secures your seat.

The Window

  • Bookings open 21 days in advance.
  • Bookings close 12 hours before dinner so I can prepare the food.

The Pivot (Rescheduling)

  • One penalty-free reschedule, no matter how much notice you give.
  • Reschedule again after that? Cancellation terms kick in.

The Flake (Cancellations)

  • Deposits are forfeited if you cancel within 24 hours of your session.

The Clock (Punctuality)

  • Please arrive on time — a 15-minute grace period before the first course is served.

The Details (Allergies)

  • Share dietary requirements/allergies when you book, not after.
  • Common allergens: dairy, gluten, nuts, soy, seafood.

The Heads-Up (Liability & Food Safety)

  • Home-based kitchen in a private residence — booking means accepting that.
  • Disclosed allergies accommodated to the best of ability; final call on safety is yours.

The Spotlight (Photography & Social Media)

  • Sessions are documented for eightysix's social media and marketing.
  • Let me know beforehand if you'd rather not be filmed/photographed.
#reservations

The Contacts

Not really a form-fill kind of business. WhatsApp me if you want a fast answer, follow the Instagram if you're still browsing, or just book straight off the calendar if you already know what you want.
